patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting clerk. Open with: “I’ve been getting more unsteady on my feet and I’m scared I’m going to fall.” Volunteer only these: bumping into doorways/furniture, clumsy hands (buttons/writing), and that it’s slowly worsened over a couple years. If asked about other symptoms, disclose: mild slurred speech when tired, coughing/choking with water/pills, intention tremor when reaching, worse in the dark/uneven ground, near-falls and one fall without passing out, eyes “jump” when looking sideways/reading dizziness, and lightheadedness on standing.

Do not offer unless specifically asked about lifestyle, sleep, driving, or daily impact: avoiding stairs/crowds, driving less, fatigue from concentrating, poor foot position sense at night, vivid dreams/talking/moving in sleep.

Resist/hedge unless directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: urinary accidents, sexual dysfunction, increased alcohol use (admit with shame if asked).

Express misconceptions: think inner ear issue; worry about Parkinson’s, low B12, or stress/anxiety.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more. Ask about costs/insurance, work, driving, and fear of needing devices.

Family history consistent with inherited ataxia (often autosomal dominant) OR known pathogenic SCA genetic test
No evidence of metabolic/toxic/infectious cause on available labs (CBC/CMP not suggestive of alternative etiology)

Discuss family planning implications and offer genetic counseling for patient and at-risk relatives

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute infectious gastroenteritis with hypovolemic dehydration causing pre-renal azotemia
acceptable_alternatives: Pre-renal acute kidney injury due to volume depletion
required_evidence: History of acute onset vomiting and/or diarrhea with poor oral intake
Clinical dehydration (e.g., orthostatic symptoms, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
Laboratory pattern consistent with volume depletion: elevated BUN out of proportion to electrolytes (pre-renal azotemia)
No red flags suggesting invasive infection or surgical abdomen (e.g., no severe focal abdominal tenderness, no peritoneal signs, no persistent high fever, no bloody stools)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Mildly low to mildly high depending on free water balance; often normal to mildly low
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low (GI losses) or normal; may be low if significant diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often low with vomiting; may be normal/low with mixed losses
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low with diarrhea (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis) OR high with predominant vomiting (metabolic alkalosis); commonly low if diarrhea prominent

non_pharmacological: If unable to tolerate PO or signs of moderate/severe dehydration: administer isotonic IV fluids (0.9% normal saline) bolus 1-2 L then reassess vitals/urine output
Avoid anti-motility agents if fever or bloody diarrhea is present; if non-bloody and afebrile, consider loperamide per standard dosing
Diet as tolerated (start with bland foods); avoid alcohol and high-fat foods until improved
Infection control: hand hygiene; avoid preparing food for others until symptom-free for at least 48 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same day/ED if worsening, unable to keep fluids down, syncope, confusion, or decreased urine output).

Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless specific indications develop (e.g., dysentery, severe disease, high-risk host)
Review penicillin allergy history and ensure no penicillin-class antibiotics are prescribed unless clearly indicated with appropriate alternatives
Discuss when to seek urgent care: inability to keep fluids down, fainting, minimal urine output, severe weakness, bloody stools, worsening pain
Oral rehydration technique (small frequent sips; replace ongoing losses)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman. You sound anxious and a bit vague, often saying “I don’t know, I’m just always hungry.” Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I keep gaining weight no matter what I do.” Volunteer: constant hunger/food thoughts, steady weight gain for a year, daytime sleepiness (could nap anywhere), leg stiffness/tripping. If asked about bowels/temperature/skin: constipation for days, don’t sweat much, overheat easily, stress skin-picking sores. Mention routine changes trigger big outbursts; lifelong low strength; small hands/feet.

Do NOT volunteer: never feeling full, eating fast/choking, low pain sensation, low libido/fertility “low hormones,” sleepy after carbs, slow wound healing.

Resist/shame topics unless directly asked gently: sneaking/hiding food, taking others’ food/lying, stool staining accidents, being controlling around food.

Misconceptions to state: “My thyroid is shot,” “pinched nerve,” worry about diabetes; admit using OTC diet pills/energy drinks.

If clinician is empathic, you soften, admit embarrassment, and ask about costs/insurance and fear of losing benefits.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to pathogenic point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region
acceptable_alternatives: Syndromic obesity with hypothalamic dysfunction consistent with Prader-Willi syndrome
required_evidence: Genetic confirmation consistent with PWS (e.g., methylation analysis/sequence identifying pathogenic point mutation in the PWS critical region)
Clinical phenotype compatible with PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking, obesity risk, developmental delay/learning difficulties, hypotonia history, hypogonadism, short stature)

expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated, consistent with insulin resistance (may be normal in leaner patients)

expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal to low (low Free T4 with non-elevated TSH suggests central hypothyroidism)

medications: Somatropin 0.24 mg/kg/week subcutaneous daily (typical starting pediatric dose; titrate to keep IGF-1 within age/sex reference range after appropriate pre-treatment evaluation)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (only if Free T4 is low or central hypothyroidism diagnosed; titrate to Free T4 in upper half of reference range in central hypothyroidism)
Metformin 500 mg PO daily with food, increase as tolerated to 1000 mg PO twice daily (if prediabetes/diabetes or significant insulin resistance present)
Hydrocortisone stress-dose regimen (e.g., 50 mg/m^2/day PO divided q6h during significant illness/surgery) only if adrenal insufficiency is confirmed or strongly suspected pending urgent testing
non_pharmacological: Structured food environment: strict caloric supervision, locked food access if needed, consistent meal plan with dietitian support tailored to PWS (lower-calorie, nutrient-dense diet)
Daily physical activity program with family/caregiver involvement; physical therapy as indicated for hypotonia/strength
Behavioral management plan for hyperphagia and rigidity; consider psychology/behavioral therapy and caregiver training
Sleep evaluation (screen for obstructive sleep apnea) and treat before/while initiating growth hormone therapy; repeat assessment if symptoms develop/worsen
Endocrine surveillance: periodic screening for diabetes, thyroid function, scoliosis, hypogonadism management options (sex steroid replacement when appropriate), and bone health
Multidisciplinary care coordination (endocrinology, nutrition, sleep medicine, behavioral health, genetics)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-12 weeks after initiating or adjusting endocrine therapy (earlier if symptomatic), then every 3-6 months for growth/metabolic monitoring; labs (IGF-1, glucose/A1c, thyroid) per therapy and risk

must_include: PWS is a genetic imprinting disorder; point mutation can disrupt expression of paternal genes in 15q11-q13
Core issues include hyperphagia, altered satiety, developmental/behavioral features, and endocrine dysfunction risks
The condition is lifelong; management focuses on structured environment and monitoring/treating complications
Rationale for growth hormone therapy (body composition, growth, strength) and need for monitoring IGF-1 and side effects
When thyroid replacement is needed (based on Free T4/TSH pattern) and how dosing is monitored
Approach to glucose abnormalities (diet/activity first, metformin if indicated) and targets for follow-up labs
Plan for evaluating/handling possible adrenal insufficiency, including when stress-dose steroids are required
Discuss sleep apnea risk and why sleep evaluation is important, especially with growth hormone

Review potential adverse effects: GH (edema, intracranial hypertension symptoms, glucose changes), metformin (GI upset, rare lactic acidosis risk in severe illness/renal failure), levothyroxine (over/under-replacement symptoms)
Confirm allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and avoidance strategies in clinical settings
Food security plan: consistent rules, controlled access, and caregiver alignment to reduce conflict and prevent overeating
Importance of regular activity and structured routines for behavior and weight management

Red flags requiring urgent care: severe lethargy, vomiting with severe headache/vision changes (possible intracranial hypertension), breathing pauses/snoring worsening, signs of adrenal crisis during illness

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for PWS management: hyperphagia/weight trajectory, sleep symptoms, developmental/behavioral concerns, endocrine symptoms (hypothyroid, hypogonadism), diabetes symptoms, adrenal insufficiency risk, current meds/supplements, recent illness, and family goals.
Perform critical allergy check before any prescribing; confirm reported penicillin urticarial rash and latex contact dermatitis and document avoidance.

Order baseline/monitoring labs relevant to PWS endocrine/metabolic risks: IGF-1 (for GH monitoring), Free T4 and TSH (central hypothyroidism screening), fasting glucose and/or HbA1c (glucose abnormalities), CMP/renal function (metformin safety), lipid panel, and consider morning cortisol/ACTH if adrenal insufficiency evaluation is planned.

Before any additional diagnostic ordering (e.g., sleep study referral, imaging if headache/vision symptoms), perform required contraindication check for the intended order context per benchmark safety requirements.
Retrieve ordered lab results to support decisions on GH initiation/continuation monitoring, thyroid replacement need, and glucose management.

State/confirm Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation (imprinting-related disruption of paternal gene expression in 15q11-q13) and identify current complications/active problems (obesity risk, sleep apnea risk, endocrine dysfunction, glucose intolerance/diabetes risk).
Explain diagnosis and lifelong management approach; set expectations and outline multidisciplinary care and monitoring.

Explain treatment options and monitoring: GH rationale and required monitoring (IGF-1, side effects, sleep evaluation), thyroid replacement indications based on Free T4/TSH, glucose management (lifestyle then metformin if indicated), and adrenal insufficiency evaluation and stress-dose steroid indications.
If prescribing is planned, check drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected) among proposed therapies (e.g., metformin, levothyroxine, other concurrent meds).

Prescribe indicated therapies only after completing critical safety checks (allergy and contraindication checks). Examples: levothyroxine for low Free T4 (esp. with normal/low TSH), metformin for confirmed dysglycemia when appropriate; GH typically initiated/managed by endocrinology with required screening/monitoring.

Address concerns and adverse effects: sleep apnea risk and importance of sleep evaluation (especially with GH), GH adverse effects (edema, intracranial hypertension symptoms, glucose changes), metformin risks (GI upset, rare lactic acidosis in severe illness/renal failure), levothyroxine over/under-replacement symptoms; confirm allergies and avoidance strategies.

Lifestyle education: food security plan (controlled access, consistent rules, caregiver alignment), activity and structured routines, and urgent red flags (severe lethargy, vomiting with severe headache/vision changes, worsening snoring/apneas, adrenal crisis signs during illness).

Arrange follow-up for lab review and monitoring targets (IGF-1 titration/monitoring if on GH, thyroid labs after dose changes, repeat A1c/glucose, weight/BMI, sleep study results) and coordinate endocrinology/sleep medicine/dietitian/behavioral supports.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and follow-up.

Mildly elevated or normal CK and aldolase (not markedly elevated as in inflammatory myopathy)
Family history compatible with autosomal dominant distal myopathy (if present) or lack of alternative cause

Confirmation by genetic testing for TTN pathogenic variant (recommended)

must_include: This is a genetic muscle condition causing slowly progressive weakness, typically affecting distal muscles of the upper limbs first
It is not an infection and not caused by nerve damage; sensation is usually preserved
Labs like CK can be normal or only mildly elevated, so normal labs do not rule it out
Confirmatory testing often requires neuromuscular evaluation and genetic testing (TTN)
There is no curative medication; treatment focuses on preserving function and managing symptoms
PT/OT and assistive devices are key to maintaining independence
Pain control options and safe dosing limits (e.g., acetaminophen maximum daily dose)

Discuss family implications and option for genetic counseling/testing

required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental disorder with Rett-like phenotype (loss of acquired purposeful hand skills and/or spoken language, gait abnormalities, hand stereotypies) with atypical course (not all classic criteria met)
Predominantly affects a female patient (or compatible presentation in male with MECP2 variant)
History of developmental regression after a period of apparent normal or mildly delayed development
Supportive features may include seizures, breathing irregularities, scoliosis, growth failure, sleep disturbance, autonomic symptoms
Genetic confirmation with pathogenic/likely pathogenic MECP2 variant (or other Rett-related genes if MECP2 negative, e.g., CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype)

If asked about associated symptoms, mention: heart racing with small exertion, lightheaded on standing, brain fog and trouble concentrating at work, irritability, sore cracks at mouth corners, and craving to chew ice. Do not offer hidden details unless specifically asked about urine/vitamins, supplements/iron, sleep breathing, bruising, or tingling: then disclose darker urine after certain vitamins, OTC iron for weeks without improvement, family noticing faster breathing during sleep, easier bruising, and nighttime foot tingling. Resist discussing low libido/erection issues unless directly asked; then admit embarrassment. Minimize weekend alcohol use and say you’d rather not have it documented. Express fear it’s neurological (clumsiness, unsteady, hand tremor, skin bumps) and mention you googled scary things. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and ask about cost/insurance and missing work for tests.

Bone marrow aspirate (Prussian blue stai.value: Ring sideroblasts ~35% of erythroid precursors; erythroid hyperplasia

confounders: Regular alcohol use (2-4 drinks nightly) which can cause/accentuate acquired sideroblastic anemia
Intermittent use of over-the-counter zinc supplements (risk of copper deficiency anemia mimicking sideroblastic patterns)
Family history of 'iron problems' leading to prior self-directed iron supplementation (can worsen iron overload and obscure iron studies interpretation)

correct_diagnosis.primary: X-linked sideroblastic anemia (ALAS2-related congenital sideroblastic anemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital sideroblastic anemia (likely ALAS2-related) pending confirmatory testing
Microcytic anemia with suspected sideroblastic etiology
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with microcytic anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with low MCV and low MCH)
Inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte count for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
RDW often increased (anisocytosis) in sideroblastic processes
Clinical context compatible with congenital/X-linked anemia (male patient, lifelong or early-onset anemia, family history suggestive of X-linked inheritance) OR prior documentation of ALAS2 mutation

Exclusion of common causes of microcytosis when possible (e.g., iron deficiency/thalassemia) and/or prior marrow evidence of ring sideroblasts if available (not required in this limited-labs task but should be considered for confirmation)

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Normal to decreased (may be relatively preserved compared with degree of anemia)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Decreased (microcytosis) or occasionally low-normal; congenital X-linked forms are commonly microcytic
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily, titrate up to 200 mg PO daily based on response and tolerability
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (support erythropoiesis)
non_pharmacological: Arrange hematology referral for confirmation (iron studies, peripheral smear review, consider bone marrow for ring sideroblasts if unclear, and genetic testing for ALAS2)

Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is demonstrated; assess for iron overload risk and consider monitoring ferritin/transferrin saturation (sideroblastic anemia can cause iron loading)
If symptomatic or severe anemia: plan for transfusion support per symptoms/hemodynamics and hematology guidance; consider iron chelation if transfusion-dependent with iron overload
Review and avoid potential marrow-toxic exposures/agents that can worsen sideroblastic anemia (e.g., alcohol, lead exposure; medication review as applicable)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and reticulocyte count in 4-6 weeks after starting pyridoxine (earlier if symptomatic); hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks

Do not start iron unless iron deficiency is proven; discuss iron overload risk in sideroblastic anemia
When transfusion would be considered (symptoms/severity) and potential need for iron monitoring/chelation if transfusion-dependent
Review allergy to penicillin and reassure that planned therapy is not a penicillin antibiotic
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation (chest pain, syncope, worsening shortness of breath, tachycardia, severe fatigue)
Discuss potential side effects of higher-dose pyridoxine (e.g., neuropathy with prolonged high doses) and to report numbness/tingling
Avoid excess alcohol and minimize exposure risks that can worsen anemia (e.g., lead)

Confirm pattern consistent with sideroblastic anemia: microcytosis with low hemoglobin and inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte response; assess iron status (often normal/high) and exclude iron deficiency before any iron therapy.

Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; specifically verify penicillin allergy details and confirm planned medication is not a penicillin antibiotic.
Check for relevant interactions before prescribing pyridoxine and/or any supportive meds; document absence of major interactions or mitigation plan.

Initiate trial of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) given potential responsiveness in X-linked sideroblastic anemia; avoid empiric iron unless iron deficiency proven; outline transfusion criteria if symptomatic/severe.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, rationale from labs, inherited nature, confirmatory testing; explain treatment timeline/monitoring; discuss iron overload risk; counsel on red flags; pyridoxine neuropathy risk; lifestyle guidance (alcohol/lead avoidance, nutrition, follow-up).

Arrange hematology referral and plan repeat CBC/reticulocytes after pyridoxine trial (and iron studies monitoring if overload risk/transfusions); set timeframe and contingency plan for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after safety checks, treatment plan, education, and follow-up are completed.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative 12-year-old 7th-grade girl who speaks clearly. Start by saying your joints keep swelling and hurting and it’s harder to do normal stuff. Volunteer: swollen painful knees and wrists that come and go for a long time, morning stiffness >1 hour, trouble running in gym/keeping up, and feeling tired even after sleep.

If asked, add: ankles/fingers sometimes puffy, occasional limp with a hot swollen knee, worse when sick or after busy days, low fevers, mild weight loss/low appetite, and sometimes sore light-sensitive eyes.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: avoiding writing/scissors, waking at night from joint pain, jaw stiffness with chewing, back/hip stiffness after sitting, and feeling irritable/down.

Resist sharing unless asked directly about bathroom issues: you sometimes hold pee because walking hurts and had a couple accidents at school.

Express misconceptions: you wonder if it’s growing pains, food allergy/gluten, or a sports injury.

Bring up money worries if treatment comes up: fear lifelong costs; think PT/braces are optional; believe daily prednisone is cheaper/better. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and ask practical cost/coverage questions.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 47-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep getting this tight feeling in my chest and I’m short of breath, especially when I walk up stairs.” Volunteer only: squeezing tightness in the middle of your chest that comes and goes, getting winded with normal activity, and unusual fatigue for 2 weeks. If asked directly, admit: it can spread to left shoulder/jaw, comes with exertion and improves with rest, occasional nausea, sweating/clamminess, palpitations, ankle swelling by evening, waking at night short of breath needing extra pillows, and an on/off dry cough. Do not mention unless specifically probed: 6–8 lb weight gain, tighter rings/shoes, less daytime urination but more at night, sour/burning throat after meals, dull upper-belly pressure. Resist disclosing cocaine use, inconsistent meds, or cost issues; deny at first (“not really”) and minimize, then admit only if asked in a nonjudgmental way. Misattribute symptoms to heartburn, a pulled muscle, or anxiety. Worry about work notes, copays, and insurance; say generics are weaker. If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and share a bit more but stay guarded.

confounders: Recent NSAID use with possible contribution to acute kidney injury

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute bacterial meningitis (suspected pneumococcal/meningococcal) requiring immediate empiric therapy
acceptable_alternatives: Sepsis of unknown source with suspected CNS infection
Viral meningitis/encephalitis (to be ruled out; treat empirically for HSV if encephalitic features)
Subarachnoid hemorrhage (rule out if thunderclap headache/meningismus)
required_evidence: Fever with severe headache and/or neck stiffness and/or altered mental status
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP and/or procalcitonin consistent with bacterial infection)
CBC supportive of infection (often leukocytosis; may be normal early)
Clinical urgency: do not delay antibiotics for LP if unstable or imaging needed
Blood cultures obtained prior to antibiotics if feasible without delaying treatment

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated with neutrophil predominance (may be normal early or in immunocompromised)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: May be normal or low if evolving sepsis/DIC
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Elevated (supports bacterial etiology; higher values increase likelihood of bacterial meningitis/sepsis)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low (SIADH can occur in CNS infections)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal unless dehydration/renal dysfunction present
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Usually normal; may track with sodium changes

medications: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days (give before or with first antibiotic dose)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (or cefotaxime 2 g IV q4-6h) empiric
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (dose-adjust to levels/renal function) empiric
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (add if age ≥50, pregnant, immunocompromised, or alcoholism to cover Listeria)
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h PRN fever/pain (max daily dose per hepatic status)
non_pharmacological: Immediate sepsis/meningitis protocol: ABCs, oxygen as needed, IV access, hemodynamic monitoring
Obtain blood cultures x2 before antibiotics if this does not delay therapy
Urgent lumbar puncture for CSF studies when safe; if focal neuro deficits, new seizures, papilledema, or significant altered mental status, obtain head CT first but do not delay antibiotics
Droplet precautions until meningococcal infection excluded or ≥24 hours after effective antibiotics if suspected
IV fluids for hypotension/dehydration; vasopressors if septic shock per protocol
Consider ICU admission if altered mental status, hemodynamic instability, or respiratory compromise
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour after initiation of empiric therapy; daily review with de-escalation when culture/CSF results return (typically within 24-72 hours), and adjust duration based on organism and clinical course

must_include: This is a medical emergency: suspected bacterial meningitis can worsen quickly
Need for immediate antibiotics even before all test results are back
Plan for blood cultures and spinal fluid testing (lumbar puncture) when safe
Empiric IV antibiotics cover the most dangerous likely bacteria; regimen will be narrowed when results return
Dexamethasone is used early to reduce risk of neurologic complications (especially pneumococcal meningitis)
Potential side effects: allergic reactions, kidney effects (vancomycin), diarrhea/C. difficile risk, steroid effects (glucose, mood)
Clarify sulfonamide (TMP-SMX) allergy and confirm no known beta-lactam allergy
Explain infection control (droplet precautions) and rationale
Discuss warning signs requiring escalation: worsening confusion, seizures, breathing difficulty, persistent hypotension
Importance of completing the full antibiotic course and attending follow-up
Household/close-contact considerations if meningococcal disease suspected (public health notification and prophylaxis for contacts as indicated)
Post-discharge monitoring for neurologic sequelae (hearing changes, cognitive issues) and when to seek urgent care

tool_call_sequence.description: Rapidly assess meningitis red flags and stability: fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, confusion, seizure, rash, recent URI/otitis/sinusitis, immunocompromise, pregnancy, recent neurosurgery/head trauma, anticoagulant use; screen for need for immediate resuscitation/ICU.

Initiate time-critical diagnostics without delaying treatment: blood cultures x2 prior to antibiotics if feasible, CBC, CMP/creatinine, lactate, coagulation studies if LP planned, CRP/procalcitonin if used locally, bedside glucose; consider HIV test if risk; pregnancy test if applicable.

Order neuroimaging (head CT) only if indicated before LP (e.g., focal neurologic deficit, new seizure, papilledema, immunocompromised state, markedly altered mental status) to reduce herniation risk; do not delay antibiotics for imaging.
Critical safety check before prescribing: clarify reported sulfonamide (TMP-SMX) allergy reaction type/severity and confirm no beta-lactam allergy; document anaphylaxis/SCAR history.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: assess interactions (e.g., nephrotoxic combinations with vancomycin, warfarin interactions, serotonergic meds if using linezolid as alternative).

Begin empiric IV therapy immediately after cultures if possible (do not wait for LP): dexamethasone given before/with first antibiotic dose; empiric antibiotics covering likely pathogens (e.g., ceftriaxone or cefotaxime + vancomycin; add ampicillin if older/immunocompromised/pregnant for Listeria coverage).

Proceed with lumbar puncture and CSF studies when safe: opening pressure, cell count/diff, protein, glucose (paired serum glucose), Gram stain/culture, PCR/NAAT panel as available; consider CSF lactate; send additional tests based on risk (cryptococcal antigen, AFB, VZV/HSV).
Review blood culture/CSF and key labs to confirm diagnosis, assess severity, and support targeted therapy; monitor renal function for vancomycin dosing and overall organ dysfunction.

Document suspected bacterial meningitis (medical emergency) and differential (viral meningitis/encephalitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, brain abscess) based on presentation and early results; identify need for droplet precautions until meningococcal disease excluded/treated.

Meet communication milestones: explain emergency nature, immediate antibiotics before all tests, plan for cultures/LP; explain empiric regimen, dexamethasone rationale, side effects; address sulfa allergy and confirm beta-lactam tolerance; explain droplet precautions; warning signs needing escalation; lifestyle/follow-up and contact prophylaxis considerations.

Plan inpatient monitoring and post-discharge follow-up: hearing evaluation if indicated, neurocognitive monitoring, completion of antibiotic course, and public health coordination if meningococcal disease suspected/confirmed.
Close encounter after ensuring treatment started, safety checks completed, and patient/family understanding and escalation plan documented.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old female teaching assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t get this nosebleed to stop, and I’m bruising really easily.” Volunteer early: nosebleed on-and-off today that takes a long time to stop; new unexplained bruises on arms/legs; gums bleed when brushing. If asked about other bleeding, share: very heavy, longer periods; small cuts ooze долго; more bleeding after dental cleanings; bleeding problems since childhood; a family member has a bleeding issue. Do not mention unless specifically probed: prolonged bleeding and big bruise after a vaccine shot; deep sore bruises lasting weeks; feeling tired/run down especially after periods; avoiding contact sports. Only reveal if asked about sexual/gynecologic bleeding or if trust is established: bleeding after sex a couple times, worried/embarrassed. Offer misconceptions: you started ibuprofen this week and think it “thins blood”; stress/poor sleep or vitamin deficiency might explain bruising; assume cheapest fix is vitamin K and worry doctors will push expensive treatments; concerned frequent labs/specialists will be unaffordable and you might space visits. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and become more willing to share sensitive details.

medications: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) 5 mg PO once daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily during menses (up to 5 days)
confounders: Recent 7-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for UTI (can raise INR/bleeding risk if vitamin K-dependent factors are borderline)
Intermittent poor dietary intake due to restrictive dieting (can mimic vitamin K deficiency and worsen PT/INR)
Occasional NSAID use for dysmenorrhea (adds platelet dysfunction-related bleeding despite normal platelet count)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital prothrombin (Factor II) deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital combined vitamin K–dependent clotting factor deficiency (if multiple VK-dependent factors are low)
required_evidence: Prolonged PT/INR out of proportion to aPTT (aPTT may be normal or mildly prolonged)
Low prothrombin (Factor II) activity level
Mixing study (PT 1:1 immediate) corrects toward normal, consistent with factor deficiency rather than inhibitor
Normal thrombin time (TT) and normal fibrinogen (Clauss), supporting a non-fibrinogen, non-thrombin inhibitor process

expected_lab_results.Mixing study (aPTT, 1:1 mix immediate): If aPTT prolonged, corrects; otherwise unchanged/normal

medications: Prothrombin complex concentrate (4-factor PCC) 25 units/kg IV once, then reassess PT/INR and bleeding; repeat dosing per hematology if ongoing bleeding
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 10-15 mL/kg IV once if PCC unavailable or contraindicated; repeat guided by PT/INR and clinical bleeding
Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) 10 mg PO/IV once only if concomitant vitamin K deficiency is suspected (e.g., poor intake, malabsorption, cholestasis) or diagnosis uncertain
non_pharmacological: Hematology consultation for confirmation, genotype/phenotype correlation, and peri-procedural planning
Bleeding precautions: avoid aspirin/NSAIDs, avoid contact sports/high-risk activities, use soft toothbrush, prompt evaluation for head injury
Peri-procedural plan: factor replacement (PCC or FFP) to target hemostatic PT/INR prior to surgery/dental work; coordinate with hematology/anesthesia
Consider antifibrinolytic adjunct for mucosal bleeding (e.g., tranexamic acid) if clinically indicated and not contraindicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck PT/INR and clinical bleeding within 1-6 hours after factor replacement in acute setting; outpatient hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for definitive management plan and education

must_include: This is a rare inherited bleeding disorder due to low/abnormal Factor II (prothrombin)
Why PT/INR is prolonged and how mixing study correction supports a factor deficiency
Bleeding risk patterns (easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, surgical bleeding) and when to seek urgent care
Goal is to replace the missing clotting factor during bleeding or before procedures (PCC preferred; FFP alternative)
Vitamin K (phylloquinone) does not correct congenital Factor II deficiency unless there is concurrent vitamin K deficiency
Potential risks of factor replacement (thrombosis risk with PCC, volume overload/allergic reactions with FFP) and need for monitoring
Review penicillin allergy (rash) and document; clarify that proposed hemostatic products are not penicillin
Discuss family implications (possible inheritance) and offer family screening/genetic counseling as appropriate
Shared plan for what to do during bleeding episodes and how to inform other clinicians/dentists
Avoid medications that increase bleeding (aspirin/NSAIDs) unless specifically advised
Carry medical alert identification and a written emergency plan
Notify healthcare teams before any procedure; coordinate prophylaxis with hematology

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current joint symptoms (pain/swelling/stiffness, morning stiffness duration), functional impact, systemic symptoms (fever, rash, weight loss), prior treatments/response, and red flags for infection or malignancy.

Clarify medication history and risk factors relevant to therapy: prior NSAID use, GI bleed/ulcer history, kidney disease, liver disease, asthma/NSAID sensitivity, pregnancy/sexual activity in adolescents (if applicable), vaccination status, infection exposures (TB/hepatitis risk).
Perform required safety check before prescribing; specifically verify and document amoxicillin-clavulanate rash details and any other drug allergies.

Order baseline labs prior to disease-modifying therapy and to assess inflammation/organ function: CBC with differential, CMP (AST/ALT, bilirubin, albumin, creatinine), ESR/CRP; consider hepatitis B/C screening and TB testing per local protocol before immunosuppression.
Review baseline results to ensure safe initiation (no significant cytopenias, transaminitis, renal impairment) and to support inflammatory arthritis assessment.

State diagnosis consistent with juvenile chronic inflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 defect; briefly explain why alternative diagnoses are less likely given available labs (no pattern suggesting cyclic hematopoiesis or sideroblastic anemia).

Perform required drug interaction check before prescribing meloxicam, methotrexate, folic acid, and any bridging steroid; verify no interacting meds (e.g., other NSAIDs, nephrotoxins, significant hepatotoxins) and document.

Perform required contraindication check before prescribing: NSAID contraindications (active GI bleed, severe renal disease), methotrexate contraindications (pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe cytopenias, significant renal impairment, active serious infection).

Initiate therapy plan: NSAID (meloxicam) for symptom control, methotrexate weekly as DMARD, folic acid supplementation; consider only a short steroid bridge for severe symptoms while awaiting DMARD effect.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment roles/timeline, missed-dose instructions for methotrexate, side effects/monitoring, allergy documentation, lifestyle/therapy, infection precautions, and return precautions.
Arrange rheumatology follow-up and lab monitoring plan (e.g., CBC/CMP at intervals after starting methotrexate) and assess response/adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and safety-netting provided.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia due to medical condition (if supported by labs/history)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Sleep disturbance causes daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood or cognitive impairment, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Present for at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or specify shorter duration if acute)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder, substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Typically within reference range (no evidence of uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia/fatigue unless history suggests otherwise)

medications: Do not prescribe carbromal; discontinue/avoid due to unfavorable safety profile and dependence/CNS depression risk

Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes and return when sleepy
Sleep diary for 1-2 weeks to guide CBT-I and track response
Assess and address contributing factors: pain, GERD, nocturia, anxiety/depression, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, symptom response, and adverse effects; sooner (within 1 week) if significant daytime sedation, falls risk, or worsening mood/suicidality

must_include: Insomnia is a disorder of sleep initiation/maintenance with daytime impact

CBT-I is first-line and more durable than sedative medications
If medication is used, it should be lowest effective dose for the shortest duration with clear goals
Carbromal should be avoided/discontinued due to safety concerns (CNS depression, dependence, impaired driving/falls, overdose risk)
Review current substance use (alcohol, cannabis, OTC antihistamines) and risks when combined with sleep aids
Warn about next-day impairment; avoid driving/operating machinery if drowsy
Set expectations: gradual improvement over weeks; follow-up plan and when to seek urgent help (severe confusion, breathing issues, suicidal thoughts)
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep
Caffeine/alcohol timing and screen/light exposure guidance
Stimulus control steps and use of a sleep diary

Before prescribing, check interactions with current meds/substances (alcohol, cannabis, OTC antihistamines, opioids/benzos, etc.); critical safety check.

Before prescribing, screen contraindications (e.g., pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment for some agents, untreated sleep apnea, severe respiratory disease, history of substance use disorder, fall risk); critical safety check.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 42-year-old woman. Open with: “I’ve had this burning when I pee and I keep needing to go all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: burning/stinging with urination, frequent urges with little output, feeling you still need to go right after, and lower belly pressure. If asked, add: cloudy/strong-smelling urine, one or two episodes of pinkish urine, pain worse at the end of urinating, no real fever but feeling run down, mild nausea but eating, started 2 days ago and worsening. Do not mention unless specifically asked about habits/sex: you’ve been holding urine at work, drinking little water, had sex 1–2 days before onset, and don’t always pee after sex. If asked about vaginal symptoms/STI risk, be somewhat hesitant but answer: unusual discharge, discomfort during sex, new partner, condoms not every time. Offer misconceptions: maybe scented body wash irritation, spicy food/coffee. Emphasize cost/time concerns; ask for least expensive tests/meds and minimal visits. correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute bacterial cystitis (uncomplicated urinary tract infection)

medications: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg PO BID x 5 days

non_pharmacological: Encourage oral hydration as tolerated

Urine culture if recurrent/complicated features or if symptoms persist/return within 2-4 weeks
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours if not improving; sooner for fever, flank pain, vomiting, or worsening symptoms

Prevention basics (urinate after sex, avoid spermicides if recurrent, do not hold urine for prolonged periods)
